content.problemStatement:
Chef is given a tree with NNN nodes and a positive integer KKK. Each node is assigned a value AiA_iAi‚Äč.

Chef has to divide the tree into a forest of exactly KKK trees, numbered from 111 to KKK, such that each node belongs to **exactly one** tree.

Let BiB_iBi‚Äč denote the bitwise XOR of the values of all nodes belonging to the ithi^{th}ith tree.

Find the **maximum** possible value of B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2B_2B2‚Äč &‚Ä¶\& \dots&‚Ä¶ &\&& BKB_KBK‚Äč, where &\&& denotes the bitwise AND operation.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines:

The first line contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK ‚ÄĒ the number of nodes in the tree and a positive integer as mentioned in the statement, respectively.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč, the values assigned to each node.
The next (N‚ąí1)(N-1)(N‚ąí1) lines describe the edges. The ithi^{th}ith of these (N‚ąí1)(N-1)(N‚ąí1) lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč, denoting an edge between nodes uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer, the **maximum** possible value of B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2B_2B2‚Äč &‚Ä¶\& \dots&‚Ä¶ &\&& BKB_KBK‚Äč
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚čÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: A possible way of dividing the nodes into 222 trees is [1,3,6][1,3,6][1,3,6] and [2,4,5][2,4,5][2,4,5].

B1=A1‚äēA3‚äēA6=1‚äē2‚äē5=6B_1 = A_1\oplus A_3\oplus A_6 = 1\oplus 2\oplus 5 = 6B1‚Äč=A1‚Äč‚äēA3‚Äč‚äēA6‚Äč=1‚äē2‚äē5=6
B2=A2‚äēA4‚äēA5=4‚äē2‚äē1=7B_2 = A_2\oplus A_4\oplus A_5 = 4\oplus 2\oplus 1 = 7B2‚Äč=A2‚Äč‚äēA4‚Äč‚äēA5‚Äč=4‚äē2‚äē1=7

Thus, B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2=6B_2 = 6B2‚Äč=6 &\&& 7=67 = 67=6.
Note that this is the maximum possible value of B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2B_2B2‚Äč you can obtain.
Another way of dividing is [1,2,4,5][1,2,4,5][1,2,4,5] and [3,6][3,6][3,6], which leads to the same answer.
Test case 222: A possible way of dividing the nodes into 333 trees is [1,3,6,7,8][1,3,6,7,8][1,3,6,7,8], [4][4][4], and [2,5][2,5][2,5]

B1=A1‚äēA3‚äēA6‚äēA7‚äēA8=1‚äē5‚äē9‚äē0‚äē8=5B_1 = A_1\oplus A_3\oplus A_6\oplus A_7\oplus A_8 = 1\oplus 5\oplus 9 \oplus 0\oplus 8 = 5B1‚Äč=A1‚Äč‚äēA3‚Äč‚äēA6‚Äč‚äēA7‚Äč‚äēA8‚Äč=1‚äē5‚äē9‚äē0‚äē8=5
B2=A4=14B_2 = A_4 = 14B2‚Äč=A4‚Äč=14
B3=A2‚äēA5=13‚äē2=15B_3 = A_2\oplus A_5 = 13\oplus 2 = 15B3‚Äč=A2‚Äč‚äēA5‚Äč=13‚äē2=15.

Thus, B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2B_2B2‚Äč &\&& B3=5B_3 = 5B3‚Äč=5 &\&& 141414 &\&& &\&& 15=415 = 415=4.
Note that this is the maximum possible value of B1B_1B1‚Äč &\&& B2B_2B2‚Äč &\&& B3B_3B3‚Äč you can obtain.
